catalyst.source: Well-designed 3D2D WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 microspheres with an effective interfacial contact, synthesized through facile single step hydrothermal method, for stimulating photocatalytic CO 2 reduction under visible light has been investigated.
catalyst.context: Well-designed 3D2D WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 microspheres with an effective interfacial contact, synthesized through facile single step hydrothermal method, for stimulating photocatalytic CO 2 reduction under visible light has been investigated.The direct growth of WO 3 microspheres with g-C 3 N 4 enables good interaction among the both semiconductors, enabling proficient charge carrier separation.Highest CO and CH 4 production over WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 of 145 and 133 mole g -1 h -1 was achieved, 1.91 and 4.03-fold higher than using pristine g-C 3 N 4 , respectively.This enhanced photoactivity was noticeable due to the synergistic effect with the larger interfacial contact area and proficient charge carrier separation.More importantly, CH 4 and CO production was increased by 2.51 and 1.64-fold with optimized H 2 OCO 2 feed ratio due to efficient adsorption of both the reactants.Similarly, by replacing water with H 2 , CO 2 reduction efficiency was increased by 1.5 and 2.6-fold higher for CO and CH 4 production.The photon flux also has a significant contribution in CO 2 reduction, whereas, 1.6 and 1.7fold higher CO and CH 4 production observed by increasing light intensity.The stability analysis reveals continuous production of CO and CH 4 in cycles without any obvious deactivation under both the lower and higher light intensity.This work demonstrates a new approach to construct composite heterojunction and would be beneficial for further investigation in selective CO 2 conversion to solar fuels.

catalyst.context: First, graphitic carbon nitride was synthesized by heating melamine (Sigma Aldrich AR 99 ) at 550 C for 2 h under air atmosphere according to our previous work 29.Then, the sample was cooled to room temperature naturally.The bulk material obtained was grinded to fine powder and then was dispersed in nitric acid (1 M) and stirred for 24 h.Finally, samples were washed with DI water several times to remove acid and was named as protonated graphitic carbon nitride (g-C 3 N 4 ). For the synthesis of WO 3 modified g-C 3 N 4 , hydrothermal approach was employed.Initially, 2 g of Na 2 WO 4 2H 2 O was dissolved in 20 mL water and then 0.1 g Na 2 SO 4 was added and stirred for 60 min to get a homogenous suspension.Afterwards, g-C 3 N 4 dispersed in water (20 mL) was added into suspension.The pH of the solution was adjusted below 2 by adding 1 M HCl under continuous stirring until a paleyellow suspension was formed.The suspension was transferred to autoclave and heated to 180 C for 24 h and then naturally cooled to room temperature.The product obtained was centrifuged and washed with DI water to get pH 7. The sludge obtained was oven dried at 100 C under air flow and finally calcined at 400 C for 2 h and named as WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 composite.The different amounts of WO 3 were adjusted by varying initial amounts of Na 2 WO 4 precursor.The pristine WO 3 was synthesized using the same procedure without the addition of g-C 3 N 4 .The schematic illustration for the synthesis of WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 has been demonstrated in Fig. 1. The crystal structure of newly developed samples was studied by Xray diffraction (XRD, RIGAKU), operated with Cu-K radiation at 0.154178 nm, 40 kV and 30 mA.The morphologies and distribution of elements were obtained by scanning electron microscope (FESEM, ZEISS Crossbeam 340) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, HITACHI HT7700).The elemental states of the composite were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD Shimazu).The optical properties were determined using UV-vis spectrophotometer (Cary 100 Agilent, Model G9821 A).The photoluminescence (PL) and Raman analysis were conducted (LabRAM HR Evolution, HORIBA) under 325 and 532 nm laser excitation source. The photocatalytic experiments were accompanied in a fixed bed photoreactor, made of stainless steel, according to our previous work 30.The light sources employed were 35 W HID car lamp and 300 W Xenon lamp with light intensity 20 and 100 mW cm -2 , respectively.A Pyrex glass of thickness 10 mm was used for passing the light irradiations and lamps were equipped with cooling fans to the remove lamp heat.Typically, 150 mg powder photo-catalyst was evenly introduced inside the stainless-steel chamber, equipped with a glass window at the top.The catalyst loading was varied from 50-250 mg to investigate the effect of different catalyst loadings on production rates and selectivity.The reactor was connected with a water saturator and vapors were carried by CO 2 , which is being passed through the water saturator, before entering into the reactor.The water saturator temperature was varied from 30 to 50 C to get different H 2 OCO 2 molar feed ratios (H 2 OCO 2 of 0.2, 0.4, and 0.60) in the gaseous feed mixture.Compressed CO 2 (99.99 ), N 2 (99.99 ) and H 2 (99.99 ) were regulated by mass flow controllers (MFC).For the investigation of photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 , both the gases were mixed in a gas mixture, before entering into the reactor.The CO 2 and H 2 were regulated at a flow rate 20 mL min -1 , having feed ratio 1.0.All the photocatalysts were tested without providing external heat and at atmospheric pressure.The gaseous products obtained after the reaction were analyzed using gas chromatography (GC-Agilent Technologies 6890 N, USA) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionized detector (FID).A gas-tight syringe was used for the injection of gaseous samples into the GC.The GC column employed was a Carboxen-1010 PLOT Capillary GC Column (L I.D. 30 m 0.53 mm) connected with both TCD and FID detectors.

catalyst.source: In this work, g-C 3 N 4 coupled with WO 3 to construct Z-scheme heterojunction was successfully synthesized via hydrothermal approach.
catalyst.context: In this work, g-C 3 N 4 coupled with WO 3 to construct Z-scheme heterojunction was successfully synthesized via hydrothermal approach.The new developed WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 composite shows good interfacial transfer and separation of charge carrier separation under visible light irradiation.The significantly enhanced photocatalytic CO 2 reduction to CO and CH 4 over WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 composite was achieved than using pristine g-C 3 N 4 and WO 3 samples.Under visible light irradiation, WO 3 donates electrons to g-C 3 N 4 due to their different conduction band position with the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction, enabling proficient charges separation among the semiconductors with enhanced CO 2 reduction to CO and CH 4 .In addition, effects of different parameters such as catalyst loading, types of reducing agents (H 2 and water) and light intensities were further tested to understand their effects on the performance of WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 composite photocatalyst.With the increase of catalyst loading and light intensity, production of both CO and CH 4 were enhanced due to the availability of more active sites in addition of proficient production of charge carrier and their separation.Comparatively, photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 O enables more CH 4 formation, whereas, hydrogen promoted more CO formation due to RWGS reaction.Similarly, using higher light intensity photocatalytic activity was obviously enhanced due to more generation of charge carrier, resulting in significantly improved CO and CH 4 formation.This reveals that fabricating structured WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 heterojunction composite is a promising approach for stimulating photocatalytic CO 2 conversion to solar fuels under visible light irradiations. lamp.context: In this work, g-C 3 N 4 coupled with WO 3 to construct Z-scheme heterojunction was successfully synthesized via hydrothermal approach.The new developed WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 composite shows good interfacial transfer and separation of charge carrier separation under visible light irradiation.The significantly enhanced photocatalytic CO 2 reduction to CO and CH 4 over WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 composite was achieved than using pristine g-C 3 N 4 and WO 3 samples.Under visible light irradiation, WO 3 donates electrons to g-C 3 N 4 due to their different conduction band position with the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction, enabling proficient charges separation among the semiconductors with enhanced CO 2 reduction to CO and CH 4 .In addition, effects of different parameters such as catalyst loading, types of reducing agents (H 2 and water) and light intensities were further tested to understand their effects on the performance of WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 composite photocatalyst.With the increase of catalyst loading and light intensity, production of both CO and CH 4 were enhanced due to the availability of more active sites in addition of proficient production of charge carrier and their separation.Comparatively, photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 O enables more CH 4 formation, whereas, hydrogen promoted more CO formation due to RWGS reaction.Similarly, using higher light intensity photocatalytic activity was obviously enhanced due to more generation of charge carrier, resulting in significantly improved CO and CH 4 formation.This reveals that fabricating structured WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 heterojunction composite is a promising approach for stimulating photocatalytic CO 2 conversion to solar fuels under visible light irradiations. operation mode.source: The stability analysis reveals continuous production of CO and CH 4 in cycles without any obvious deactivation under both the lower and higher light intensity.
operation mode.context: Well-designed 3D2D WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 microspheres with an effective interfacial contact, synthesized through facile single step hydrothermal method, for stimulating photocatalytic CO 2 reduction under visible light has been investigated.The direct growth of WO 3 microspheres with g-C 3 N 4 enables good interaction among the both semiconductors, enabling proficient charge carrier separation.Highest CO and CH 4 production over WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 of 145 and 133 mole g -1 h -1 was achieved, 1.91 and 4.03-fold higher than using pristine g-C 3 N 4 , respectively.This enhanced photoactivity was noticeable due to the synergistic effect with the larger interfacial contact area and proficient charge carrier separation.More importantly, CH 4 and CO production was increased by 2.51 and 1.64-fold with optimized H 2 OCO 2 feed ratio due to efficient adsorption of both the reactants.Similarly, by replacing water with H 2 , CO 2 reduction efficiency was increased by 1.5 and 2.6-fold higher for CO and CH 4 production.The photon flux also has a significant contribution in CO 2 reduction, whereas, 1.6 and 1.7fold higher CO and CH 4 production observed by increasing light intensity.The stability analysis reveals continuous production of CO and CH 4 in cycles without any obvious deactivation under both the lower and higher light intensity.This work demonstrates a new approach to construct composite heterojunction and would be beneficial for further investigation in selective CO 2 conversion to solar fuels.

For the synthesis of WO 3 modified g-C 3 N 4 , hydrothermal approach was employed.Initially, 2 g of Na 2 WO 4 2H 2 O was dissolved in 20 mL water and then 0.1 g Na 2 SO 4 was added and stirred for 60 min to get a homogenous suspension.Afterwards, g-C 3 N 4 dispersed in water (20 mL) was added into suspension.The pH of the solution was adjusted below 2 by adding 1 M HCl under continuous stirring until a paleyellow suspension was formed.The suspension was transferred to autoclave and heated to 180 C for 24 h and then naturally cooled to room temperature.The product obtained was centrifuged and washed with DI water to get pH 7. The sludge obtained was oven dried at 100 C under air flow and finally calcined at 400 C for 2 h and named as WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 composite.The different amounts of WO 3 were adjusted by varying initial amounts of Na 2 WO 4 precursor.The pristine WO 3 was synthesized using the same procedure without the addition of g-C 3 N 4 .The schematic illustration for the synthesis of WO 3 g-C 3 N 4 has been demonstrated in Fig. 1. The crystal structure of newly developed samples was studied by Xray diffraction (XRD, RIGAKU), operated with Cu-K radiation at 0.154178 nm, 40 kV and 30 mA.The morphologies and distribution of elements were obtained by scanning electron microscope (FESEM, ZEISS Crossbeam 340) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, HITACHI HT7700).The elemental states of the composite were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD Shimazu).The optical properties were determined using UV-vis spectrophotometer (Cary 100 Agilent, Model G9821 A).The photoluminescence (PL) and Raman analysis were conducted (LabRAM HR Evolution, HORIBA) under 325 and 532 nm laser excitation source. The photocatalytic experiments were accompanied in a fixed bed photoreactor, made of stainless steel, according to our previous work 30.The light sources employed were 35 W HID car lamp and 300 W Xenon lamp with light intensity 20 and 100 mW cm -2 , respectively.A Pyrex glass of thickness 10 mm was used for passing the light irradiations and lamps were equipped with cooling fans to the remove lamp heat.Typically, 150 mg powder photo-catalyst was evenly introduced inside the stainless-steel chamber, equipped with a glass window at the top.The catalyst loading was varied from 50-250 mg to investigate the effect of different catalyst loadings on production rates and selectivity.The reactor was connected with a water saturator and vapors were carried by CO 2 , which is being passed through the water saturator, before entering into the reactor.The water saturator temperature was varied from 30 to 50 C to get different H 2 OCO 2 molar feed ratios (H 2 OCO 2 of 0.2, 0.4, and 0.60) in the gaseous feed mixture.Compressed CO 2 (99.99 ), N 2 (99.99 ) and H 2 (99.99 ) were regulated by mass flow controllers (MFC).For the investigation of photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with H 2 , both the gases were mixed in a gas mixture, before entering into the reactor.The CO 2 and H 2 were regulated at a flow rate 20 mL min -1 , having feed ratio 1.0.All the photocatalysts were tested without providing external heat and at atmospheric pressure.The gaseous products obtained after the reaction were analyzed using gas chromatography (GC-Agilent Technologies 6890 N, USA) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionized detector (FID).A gas-tight syringe was used for the injection of gaseous samples into the GC.The GC column employed was a Carboxen-1010 PLOT Capillary GC Column (L I.D. 30 m 0.53 mm) connected with both TCD and FID detectors.
